title: Tapping for the Trying-Too-Hard Paradox
subtitle: A tapping practice for releasing the effort that blocks sleep
excerpt: A five-minute tapping practice from Day 22 of a 30-day sleep program. The script addresses the paradox of effortful sleep: the harder the trying, the more the body resists. The reframe names the body's own knowledge and invites release of the fixing energy.
sourceNotes: The tapping framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique (EFT), in use since the mid-1990s. The script is adapted from Day 22 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use during the day when you notice you have been treating sleep as a problem to solve, or when the habit of trying hard to sleep is creating pressure rather than rest.

content.text: A five-minute daytime tapping practice from Day 22 of a 30-day sleep program. The script targets the paradox at the centre of most sleep problems: trying harder to sleep creates tension rather than rest, and the body resists the effort. The reframe returns to the body's own capacity and names the release of trying as the effective action.
New to tapping? Read
how EFT tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Even though I've made sleep another thing to achieve, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though I read the tips and try the hacks, I honour how much I want this to get better.
Even though all that trying just makes me tighter, I'm open to letting it be simple again.
Tapping round
Eyebrow: I've been treating rest like a problem to solve.
Side of eye: The harder I try, the more my body resists.
Under eye: I can't force relaxation — it's earned by letting go.
Under nose: My body knows how to sleep; it always did.
Chin: I've just been standing in the way of it.
Collarbone: I can stop managing and start trusting.
Under arm: I'm not broken — I'm just overtired from trying too hard.
Top of head: I give myself permission to do less.
Reframe to positive
Eyebrow: My body already knows what to do.
Side of eye: Sleep is natural, not complicated.
Under eye: The more I release, the easier it comes.
Under nose: I'm allowed to stop fixing and start feeling.
Chin: Rest comes to those who let it.
Collarbone: I can be still and let my body remember.
Under arm: I give myself full permission to stop trying.
Top of head: I rest easily because I stopped efforting.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
The tapping framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique (EFT), developed in the mid-1990s. The script is adapted from Day 22 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
